coredata.dc:title: Post-epoxidation of graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yingmin
Liu, Xinqian
Kong, Wenhui
Wang, Lifeng
Li, Yan
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a simple, rapid and green post-epoxidation treatment for graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was proposed. This method only adjusted epoxy functional groups on GQDs' surface, and correspondingly maintained their original size and concentration. The formation mechanism of this epoxidation of graphene quantum dots (E-GQDs) is similar to the epoxidation of alkenes, which will be epoxidated by electrophilic addition reaction. The GQDs after epoxidation will enhanced surface activity of GQDs, and help to expand its application in biological imaging and photothermal therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Epoxy group
Post-epoxidation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of the GQDs (a) and E-GQDs (b). The histograms of size distributions of GQDs (c) and E-GQDs (d).
The UV–vis absorbtion spectrum (a) and PL spectrum (b,c) of GQDs and E-GQDs.
The XPS survey spectra (a) and the FT-IR spectra (b) as well as the Raman spectra (c) of the GQDs and E-GQDs.
The C1s (a,b) and O1s (c,d) XPS spectra of GQDs and E-GQDs.
The mature process of olefin epoxidation.
The reaction of post-epoxidation from GQDs to E-GQDs.

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
University of Science and Technology Beijing

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: No. 30, Xueyuan Rd, Haidian district, Beijing 100083, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a simple, rapid and green post-epoxidation treatment for graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was proposed. This method only adjusted epoxy functional groups on GQDs' surface, and correspondingly maintained their original size and concentration. The formation mechanism of this epoxidation of graphene quantum dots (E-GQDs) is similar to the epoxidation of alkenes, which will be epoxidated by electrophilic addition reaction. The GQDs after epoxidation will enhanced surface activity of GQDs, and help to expand its application in biological imaging and photothermal therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a novel type of fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterial, have drawn increasing attention in recent. Due to the ultrasmall size, chemical inertia, low cytotoxicity and great biocompatibility of GQDs, they have potential application in bioimaging, fluorescent sensing, light-emitting diodes, photocatalysis and organic photovoltaics, etc [1–8]. In order to deep study their properties and application, a number of synthesis methods have been reported over the past few years. In general, these synthesis can be divided into two approaches: top-down and bottom-up methods. Top-down approaches refer to the cutting of graphene sheets into GQDs, the method consists of hydrothermal [5], electrochemical oxidation [9,18], and chemical oxidation stripping carbon fiber [10]. Bottom-up methods involve the synthesis of graphene moieties containing a certain number of conjugated carbon atoms, include chemical solution routes [11–13], microwave and ultrasonic method [14–18]. Compared to bottom-up methods (which involve a series of complex organic reactions), top-down methods usually can be obtained GQDs simply and have a high yield, and thus been widely used.

GQDs obtained by top-down approach usually have some surface defects, arise from the surface unsaturated atoms and un-removed heteroatom (especially oxygen atoms) introduced from prepared process. These surface defects together with connected graphene impart GQDs novel chemical properties, such as water solubility, biocompatibility and reduced toxicity, resulting from the new formed surface functional groups. Meanwhile, these functional groups have an impact on the optical properties of GQDs because of the change of local electric field. However, the surface defects from the prepared process are random and usually out of controllable. Therefore, surface modification was proposed and used to precisely control various properties of GQDs by adjusting their surface functional groups, including using polydopamine [19], polyaniline [20], small organic molecules [21] and so on.

Among the surface modification, surface oxygen regulation is an important approach to control and improved chemical and physical properties of GQDs. Since several types of oxygen functional groups co-exist on the surface of almost top-down GQDs, including hydroxyl, epoxy, carboxyl, aldehyde, et al. [19,22] Each type of oxygen group plays a key role on their various properties, such as optical property and water solubility. Previously, many reports pointed out that the modification of single functional groups for carbon-based nanomaterials will be able to control accurately over physical or chemical properties, or endow their new properties. For example, Yin et al. [23] fabricated fullerol, a polyhydroxy derivative of fullerene, and demonstrated that it had excellent scavenging capacity for several types of free radicals. Hence, fullerol would have extremely valuable potential in applications of medicine and other fields. Saud et al. [24] fabricated CQDs/TiO2 composite nanofibers applied to photocatalytic wastewater treatment, the formation of Ti-O-C occurs through the covalent cross-linking reaction between epoxy groups on the surface of carbon quantum dots and basic groups on TiO2. Liu et al. [25] performed the single-step synthesis of polyhydroxy fluorescent carbon dots, and verified that polyhydroxy carbon dots had high sensitivity in Cr3+ detection. Nevertheless, the effective methods of controlling surface single oxygen groups on GQDs surface is lacking, due to the uncertain location of each type of oxygen groups and relatively easy convertion between different type of oxygen groups.

For epoxy groups, because their unique structure involving two carbon atoms joined to an oxygen atom, they usually produced on the surface of graphene layer, not at the edges. So, the advantage of their location make epoxy adjusting as possible. In this study, an epoxidation method of olefin was proposed using hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) solution as the oxygen source to epoxidise GQDs and explore their new application·H2O2 solution were used to achieve the epoxidation of GQDs in the ionic solution of 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate (BF4), catalyzed by aqueous sodium hydroxide. The epoxidized GQDs (E-GQDs) maintained their original size and concentration. In addition, the reaction reagents could be removed by dialysis, which prevented the introduction of hetero-atoms. Therefore, this is a versatile and simple method to achieve the control over a single type of oxygen-containing functional group on the surface of GQDs.

GQDs were prepared by electrochemical cyclic voltammetry technology using a CHI660D electrochemical workstation, as described in our previous reports [26]. Graphite rod, platinum electrode, and Ag/AgCl were used as the working, counter, and reference electrodes, respectively. 0.1 M phosphate buffer saline (PBS) solution with a pH value of 7.0 was used as the electrolyte. The applied potential window was set to −5.0 to 5.0 V, the scan rate was 0.5 V/s, and continuous conducted 129,600 cycles. After filtering by a 220 nm filter membrane and dialyzing with a cellulose ester membrane bag (retained molecular weight: 3500 Da) for 6 days, GQDs aqueous solutions were obtained, which were used for further redox treatments.

An aqueous solution of GQDs (20 mL) was poured into a 50 mL beaker, and then 200 μL (30%) of H2O2, 400 μL of BF4 ion solution and 0.2 g of NaOH were dropped into the solution. After stirring for 1 min, the mixture solution was put in constant temperature of 0 °C for 2 h. The treated GQDs was named as E-GQDs. Once E-GQDs were obtained, a dialysis process was carried out immediately.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a JEM-2010 transmission electron microscope. FT-IR spectra were recorded on a NEXUS spectrometer 670. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were measured by an ESCALAB250 Xi photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher). Raman spectra were recorded on a LabRAM HR Evolution with a 514.5 nm laser.

As illustrated in Fig. 1 a and b, the typical TEM images of GQDs and E-GQDs showed their size distribution was within the range of 3.4–4.6 nm. The particles also exhibited spherical shapes and excellent monodispersity. The mean particle diameter of E-GQDs was approximately 3.9 nm, which was similar to that of origin GQD. This implied that post-epoxidation only modified the surface of GQDs, and did not damage their intrinsic structure.

The optical properties are important features of GQDs, so the UV–vis and PL spectra of GQDs and E-GQDs have been investigated here. In Fig. 2 a, both the UV–vis spectra of GQDs and E-GQDs exhibited two typical absorption peaks, one about 225 nm and the other about 270 nm. The former one is attributable to the π−π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domain, and the latter one could be ascribed to the n−π* transition of sp3 clusters [27,28]. Because of its relatively high degree of surface oxidization, the E-GQDs presented an enhanced absorption tail at about 270 nm. And due to COC groups usually present on the basal plane, the new formed COC groups maybe transformed from two previous defect carbon atoms and one oxygen atom. The partial restored sp2 domain in basal plane lead to the increase in sp2 domain area and push the absorption peak at ca. 225 nm of E-GQDs red-shift. Both the PL spectra of GQDs and E-GQDs presented a typical excitation-dependent feature, as shown in Fig. 2b and c, with the excitation wavelength increased from 340 to 480 nm, the emission bond changed correspondingly from 440 to 520 nm. Except a weak broadening at 400–450 nm of emission bond of E-GQDs, no obvious difference between GQDs and E-GQDs in PL behavior. This weak broadening in PL spectra maybe come from intrinsic emission of restored sp2 domain of E-GQDs [29].

Fig. 3 a was the XPS spectra of GQDs and E-GQDs. The results displayed that the predominant two peaks were attributed to C1s peak (∼284.5 eV) and O1s peak (∼532.0 eV), but with different relative peak intensities. The oxygen content of GQDs was 29.04%, and that of E-GQDs was 35.10%. The increase in oxygen content also implied that oxidation occurred during the post-epoxidation process. The FT-IR spectra of GQDs and E-GQDs (Fig. 3b) showed similar types of oxygen functional groups: the absorption peak that appeared between wave numbers of 1047–1110 cm−1 was the vibration peak of COC; the peak at 1638 cm−1 corresponded to the vibration peak of CO, while the peak at 3418 cm−1 corresponded to the vibration peak of OH [30,31]. As we expected, E-GQDs showed a significantly enhanced COC vibration peak compared to GQDs, which indicated that our method had effectively controlled the epoxy groups on the surface of GQDs. The Raman spectroscopy of GQDs and E-GQDs (Fig. 2c) exhibited two strong peaks at 1366 cm−1 and 1596 cm−1, which could be attributed to a disorder induced D-band caused by phonon scattering at defect sites and a doubly degenerated G-band resulted from in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon, respectively [32,33]. Generally speaking, the intensity ratio of D- and G-peaks (ID/IG) could be used to describe the intensity relationship between these two peaks. The ID/IG ratio of E-GQDs (0.93) was significantly lower than that of GQDs (1.09), indicating the formation of large quantities of epoxy groups, which increased the area of conjugation region in GQDs and thus led to the relative increase in G-peak intensity and the decrease in ID/IG [34,35].

In order to further investigate the changes of surface oxygen groups in each GQD sample, high resolution C1s and O1s peaks in the XPS of GQDs and E-GQDs (Fig. 4 a–d) were conducted. The C1s peak could be mainly deconvoluted into four peaks at ∼284.5 eV, ∼285.8 eV, ∼287.2 eV and ∼288.6 eV, corresponding to CC/CC, COH, COC, CO respectively. The obvious increase of the COC content from 21.42% to 29.94% corresponded to the transformation from GQDs to E-GQDs. Conversely, the COH and CO contents showed small changes. The O1s peak also could be mainly deconvoluted into four peaks at ∼ 533.8 eV, ∼532.5 eV, ∼531.2 eV and ∼ 535.9 eV, corresponding to COH, COC, CO and H2O, respectively. Similar to the C1s, only the content of COC of E-GQDs have a evident promotion compare with the GQDs, and other two oxygen functional groups have no obvious change. These results further demonstrated that post-epoxidation treatment modified more COC groups on the surface of GQDs, and hence successfully controlled a single type of oxygen-containing functional group.

In summary, this study proposed a versatile and simple post-fabrication treatment method, which successfully controlled a single type of functional group on the surface of GQDs. This method not only ensured that the size of GQDs remained unchanged, it also prevented the introduction of impurities. The single control of epoxy functional groups on GQDs is conducive to more precise control of GQDs performance and its application value.

Yan Li and Yingmin Wang designed experiments and wrote the manuscript. Xin-qian Liu Wenhui Kong Lifeng Wang and Ning Ma performed the experiments. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21674011), Beijing Municipal Natural Science Foundation (2172040) and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (FRF-GF-17-B11).
